summary: This post is a proof-of-concept for a 'customer-focused blog' strategy, demonstrating how to create useful content that naturally incorporates a product recommendation. McKenzie teaches a Japanese kanji (任, meaning 'to entrust' or 'leave up to someone') while plugging ReadWrite Kanji software. The post includes readings, JLPT test vocabulary, and trivia connecting the kanji to Nintendo's name etymology. An editor's note at the end breaks the fourth wall to explain the marketing techniques being demonstrated, including calls to action.
key_points: The post is an explicit demonstration of content marketing: creating genuinely useful content that naturally incorporates a product recommendation
The kanji 任 connects to Nintendo's name (任天堂), meaning 'the house of leaving one's fortune to heaven,' derived from a poetic expression about gambling on fate
McKenzie transparently discloses his motivations, noting the post is a proof-of-concept for a blog series on customer-focused blogging
The editor's note reveals the meta-layer: the call to action at the end is itself a teaching example of effective blog marketing techniques
money_quotes: This association of submission before heaven and gambling probably had something to do with the naming of a certain playing card company 任天堂, 'the house of leaving one's fortune to heaven', which is probably better known as the company which went on to make two Italian plumbers household names in every nation on earth.
Editor's note: there won't be a kanji of the day tomorrow. Its an example of an effective call to action.
Everything which follows this disclaimer, including my representations as to my opinion of the quality of that software, is true.
key_insight: Effective content marketing creates genuinely useful content where the product recommendation feels natural rather than forced, as demonstrated by embedding a software plug within a Japanese language lesson.
